title: Service Account and Scheduled Task Review
summary: Assess whether non-human accounts, scheduled tasks, secrets, and shared credentials are owned, rotated, and monitored properly.
objective: Assess whether service account governance, scheduled task ownership, secret rotation, and shared credential controls are designed and operating effectively.
scope: Monitoring for shared or stale credentials

controls.name: Owned Service Accounts
controls.description: Non-human accounts should have named ownership and documented business purpose.

controls.name: Secret Rotation and Vaulting
controls.description: Service account secrets should be rotated on schedule and stored in an approved vault.

controls.name: Scheduled Task Review
controls.description: Scheduled tasks should be reviewed to ensure they use approved accounts and current credentials.

controls.name: Monitoring of Stale Credentials
controls.description: Stale or shared credentials should be flagged and investigated when detected in logs or inventories.

evidence.title: Service Account Inventory
evidence.content: The inventory lists 23 service accounts, but four entries use generic names like svc-app01 and do not show a named business owner.

evidence.title: Password Vault Rotation Log
evidence.content: The vault rotation log shows two service accounts overdue for password rotation by 38 days, with one rotation ticket left in 'awaiting confirmation' status.

evidence.title: Scheduled Task Export
evidence.content: Scheduled task export shows the payroll job still runs under a shared legacy account that is also used for a file transfer script on another server.

evidence.title: Service Account Standard
evidence.content: The standard requires service accounts to have named owners, approved business purposes, vault rotation, and periodic review of scheduled-task usage.

evidence.title: Monitoring Alert Summary
evidence.content: The alert summary shows repeated logins by svc-app01 from two servers outside its normal schedule, but the security team tagged the activity as a planned maintenance pattern.

evidence.title: Application Maintenance Note
evidence.content: The application owner says a legacy service account was kept in place to avoid breaking overnight jobs during a migration, although the migration closeout never explicitly renamed the owner.

issues.title: Service Accounts Without Clear Ownership
issues.description: Several non-human accounts lacked a named owner or business purpose in the inventory.
issues.recommendation: Require each service account to have a named owner, documented purpose, and periodic attestation.

issues.title: Overdue Secret Rotation
issues.description: Service account passwords were overdue for rotation and one ticket remained unfinished.
issues.recommendation: Automate secret rotation monitoring and require closure evidence for overdue rotation tickets.

issues.title: Shared Legacy Account Used by Scheduled Tasks
issues.description: A payroll task and another script shared a legacy account, weakening accountability and separation.
issues.recommendation: Assign unique service accounts to critical jobs and eliminate shared credentials where possible.

inbox.subject: Kickoff: Service Account and Scheduled Task Review
inbox.preview: Focus on whether non-human accounts are owned, rotated, and monitored like real identities.
inbox.body: This review is about the identity layer that runs behind the scenes. Look for service accounts with no real owner, stale passwords, and scheduled tasks that still rely on shared credentials.

inbox.subject: Service account extracts attached
inbox.preview: Sharing the inventory, vault log, and scheduled task export.
inbox.body: Some of these accounts are old, but they still support nightly processing. We may have kept a few account names around during migration so jobs would not fail.

interviewPrompts.question: Who owns the generic service accounts in the inventory?
interviewPrompts.answer: They were inherited during migration and some never got a clean owner assignment. We know what they do, but the naming is not as tidy as it should be.

interviewPrompts.question: Why are two secrets overdue for rotation?
interviewPrompts.answer: The rotation tickets were opened, but one was waiting on a maintenance window and the other got stuck in confirmation status after the vault change.

interviewPrompts.question: Why does the payroll task still use a shared legacy account?
interviewPrompts.answer: The job was left on the old account to keep overnight processing stable. We planned to move it later, but the migration closeout never forced the change.

interviewPrompts.question: Were the repeated logins expected maintenance activity?
interviewPrompts.answer: Maybe partially. The pattern looked familiar, but the account is used by more than one server, so the alert was not easy to interpret right away.

closeout.managementMessage: The service account environment works, but ownership and credential discipline are weaker than they should be. Shared identities and overdue rotation create avoidable control risk across critical jobs.
closeout.auditorReflection: Non-human identities are easy to ignore until a job breaks or a password ages out. The key is proving who owns them, how they are rotated, and whether logs make sense when they are used.
